adversarial-writing.title: ML Reading Notes
adversarial-writing.subtitle: Zhihu articles, surveys, and dense notes
adversarial-writing.summary: A long writing trail on adversarial examples, robustness, and interpretability, spanning public explainers, research maps, and paper notes.

preview.text: When I was doing research with Prof. Xinggang Wang, I used to do weekly paper readings and wrote detailed notes over it. It spans across general computer vision, object detection, adversarial attacks and defenses. These notes were part of the research requirement but I also enjoyed it.
The link to the old blog is [here](https://greenere.github.io/blogs.html)
I wrote a list of zhihu articles with some readings:

layoff-journey.title: Layoff Journey
layoff-journey.subtitle: An article about my first layoff experience happening in Aug 25th, 2025
layoff-journey.summary: I was laid off in August 2025.To be honest, it didn’t come completely out of nowhere. The industry had already been going through waves of layoffs, and deep down I knew it was possible. Still, when it actually happened, it felt abrupt and strangely disorienting.

preview.text: The company had apparently planned it earlier, but due to internal processes—legal reviews, coordination—it got delayed. Then one Monday, it finally happened. A Monday layoff. That alone felt off, almost symbolic of how chaotic things had become.

preview.label: 硅谷裁员潮，终究逃不掉——2025年中失业故事

oxford.title: Oxford Summer School
oxford.subtitle: Knowledge Representation Summer School, Aug. 2018
oxford.summary: A two week summer school reimbursed and encouraged by my undergraduate college. We were supposed to go to UIUC but they canceled due to some misunderstanding, so we had to all go to Oriel College in Oxford University.

preview.text: It was my first time ever been abroad. We spent two weeks in England. The courses were a good introduction, but what really impressed me was how tranquil the countryside of England feels like. We visited London and Brighton in weekends, and I also got a chance to take a guided tour to the BMW Mini factory.

hust.title: Huazhong University of Science and Technology
hust.subtitle: Bachelor's degree in Electronic and Information Engineering, 2016-2020
hust.summary: I spent four years for my undergraduate study in Wuhan, China. Originally I was in the School of Environment but later transferred to the School of Electronic Information and Communications. We have a huge campus with lush trees, making the summer feels pleasant rather than tortuous like other places in Wuhan.

preview.text: I graduated in the top 10 with a 3.91/4.0 GPA, toped the class in the end but never got to top any single semester, so it was funny.

The first two years were occupied by trying to have fun while keeping up with the courses. I did tour most places in Wuhan that I found interesting and actively participated in Poestry Association and SICA (Student International Communication Association), and tried to found a Spanish club but failed.

The thrid year came with the burden to lay bedrock for the future, as I tried to get admitted to a pre-graduate program summer school, I did not find myself lucky when other students were talking about research and projects, yet the only thing I could talk was reading.

cornell.title: Cornell University
cornell.subtitle: ECE M.Eng in Ithaca from Jan, 2021 to Dec, 2022
cornell.summary: It was a fun period when I enjoyed my life in a serene, quiet and remote college town. I did my best to explore the beautiful Upstate New York and I really enjoyed the fall. I took the last chance to take all courses I am interested, 6 of them are PE classes :)

preview.text: I was supposed to attend in 2021, but we all know what happened. The world-wide tragedy forever changed our lives, and I had to spend a gap year to do all hussles but I finally got to Ithaca in August,2022.

I lived my first year in the North Campus in a house of nine bedrooms, and had to walk ten kilometers in a daily basis since the Engineering school is in the south. The first few months were very vivid, we almost had some gatherings every week to celebrate the birthday of a classmate, or just to have fun.

The tedious job search for a summer internship came with the extremely cold winter of Ithaca later on, and I had to spend a lot of time balance between interview preparations and courseworks. I never landed a job in my first semester.

naec.title: Research at NAEC
naec.subtitle: National Anti-counterfeit Engineering Research Center
naec.summary: A part-time non-paid research internship where a course interest in adversarial examples became sustained work on handwritten signature verification and, later, a journal publication.

preview.text: This was the period when adversarial machine learning stopped being just a course topic and became a real research track. Working with Prof. Wei Yuan's group, I focused on attacking signature verification systems under realistic black-box constraints.

It was a part-time non-paid research internship during the HUST years. I primarily worked on adversarial attacks against handwritten signature verification system starting from the summer of 2019 and later worked on a face-swap system for my graduation capstone project.

hust-mclab.title: Research with Prof. Xinggang Wang
hust-mclab.subtitle: Adversarial ML research with Prof. Xinggang Wang
hust-mclab.summary: A research-assistant period focused on adversarial robustness, interpretability, and what adversarial training changes inside a model.

preview.text: This lab period deepened the adversarial-ML thread that began with the signature project. Much of the reading, experimentation, and writing around noise modulation came together here.

items: [Noise Modulation: Let your model interpret itself](https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.10603)

realai.title: Algorithm Intern at RealAI
realai.subtitle: AI security internship in Beijing, 2021
realai.summary: During my research into adversarial examples, I found this interesting company trying to put AI security into industry. So I applied and did an internship there, where I got to know many renowned researcher in this field.

preview.text: To be honest, I do not really understand what RealAI is trying to do. During my internship, the mentor asked me to think of ways to make adversarial examples for face recognition system physically viable.

The main challenge was that normally we use flat digital images for training and adversarial attacks, while in the physical world, human face is not flat. So I was exploring ways to reliably mapping the unflat face to a flat space.
Overall, I did not accomplish anything meaningful despite got to know the authors of many papers I read and admire.

verily-intern.title: Summer Intern at Verily
verily-intern.subtitle: Software Engineering Intern working on a retinal camera
verily-intern.summary: I found this internship at late March when I almost lost hope in job searching, and it started in May, so everything felt lucky. It was my first time ever been to California and my first time ever do a "real" summer internship.

preview.text: Back then, Verily Life Sciences was still a subsidiary of Alphabet and we still had access to Google buildings. So the internship feels both like working in a large company in Google and working in a startup where everything is shifty.

It was my first time in California, but I did not know how to drive. So I did not get a lot chances to explore the bay area but still managed to check out the Golden Gate Bridge, the California Coast and even made several short trips to visit my friends in Boston and Seattle.

Our team was working on a retinal camera project, which was not launched back then but were to be canceled in 2025. I worked on building a visualization tool to help the development of the retinal imaging algorithms. But work was the least thing I can remember now when I think of this summer.

verily.title: Verily
verily.subtitle: Full-time chapter beginning in 2023
verily.summary: The once long-running chapter: product engineering, system ownership, and the part of the graph that connects most directly back to the present-day bio node.

preview.text: Compared with the earlier nodes, this one reflects a steadier and less school-shaped rhythm. It is about longer projects, production responsibility, and a deeper engineering identity that can later branch into writing, side projects, or future moves.

items: Continuation of the Verily internship into a full-time role
Represents the most up-to-date experience thread in the graph
A natural bridge back to the central bio node and present tense

waymo.title: Waymo
waymo.subtitle: Full-time job since Dec 15th, 2025, still enjoying it
waymo.summary: A former colleague asked me if I am interested in Waymo after my previous project was cancelled and the entire team was laid off by Verily. Apparently I said yes and now I am working on building the labeling platform that powers the ML models in Waymo.

preview.text: I am in a team spanning across data, model and ops, a lot of new stuff to learn with a lot of new opportunities. The progress that Waymo is making is very exciting and I wish to be able to stay here until its potential IPO.

wellmail.title: WELLMAIL
wellmail.subtitle: An e-mail client with GUI written in Python
wellmail.summary: It was a course project finished with a group of three. We built a Python e-mail client with a GUI, and spent a lot of time figuring out how to make SMTP and QT work.

preview.text: Check it out here: [WELLMAIL](https://github.com/Greenere/wellmail)
This is the very first project I ever participated that could be referred as a project. For the course, Software Project, an e-mail client is required to be designed and implemented.

We were splitted into groups of three, and we had literally zero experience about how to begin. Most of the other groups chose Java to programmed it, while we chose Python after a few consideration. Python is light, new and all three members wanted to proceed into relevant areas that requires Python's help.

louvre-evacuation.title: Louvre Evacuation Model
louvre-evacuation.subtitle: ICM 2019 modeling project
louvre-evacuation.summary: A contest modeling project that used a cellular-automata-style abstraction of the Louvre to design a dynamic evacuation strategy.

preview.text: In Janurary. 2019, I led a group of three competed in the Interdisciplinary Contest in Modeling (ICM). We chose the problem designated as "D" that required us to build a model for the Louvre Museum and design an evacuation strategy for it.

It took us one day to discuss and decide an appropriate model and finally I proposed a cellular automata model for the Louvre. You may think that we represented each person to be evacuated as a cell, but actually we chose several representative exhibition halls to be the cells in our model.

weibe.title: WEIBE
weibe.subtitle: Weibo emotion analysis system
weibe.summary: A course-built system that crawled Weibo trending topics, stored posts, and visualized emotional tendencies across those conversations.

preview.text: It's publicly archived here: [WEIBE](https://github.com/Greenere/weibe)

This is a course project for the course, Capstone Project in Big Data Processing System. The core idea of this project is to fetch the trending topics of weibo (the Chinese version of Twitter) and analyze corresponding posts for the emotion tendencies concealed in each tweet.
I am responsible for the system rather than the algorithm behind the emotion analysis. The scheme I designed for this system is shown as below.

faceswap.title: FaceSwap
faceswap.subtitle: Undergraduate capstone project
faceswap.summary: A bachelor's graduation project focused on generating face-swapped short videos, with attention to the full pipeline rather than only the core model.

preview.text: This is my graduate design for my bachelor's degree. The major focus is the generation of deepfakes, more specificly, face swapped videos. I adjust the existing method with some minor improvements for generation part. The detection of deepfakes appears as a survey in the final paper.
You can find it here on my github: [faceswap](https://github.com/Greenere/faceswap/tree/master)
It takes a lot of work for auxiliary modules to construct an end-to-end pipeline as shown below:

data-visualizations-2021.title: Data Visualizations
data-visualizations-2021.subtitle: Interactive D3 projects during Cornell period
data-visualizations-2021.summary: I attended this interesting course INFO 5100 at Cornell, where we were taught the principals of data visualizations and how to use d3.js to realize those ideas. There were three course projects that are very interesting.

preview.text: The first project was a static course chart showing the course distributions of three adjacent majors in Cornell over a week, where we can see the temporal and spatial concentrations of courses.
The second project was an interactive kitchen board called "Online Kitchen", where you can drag your food and see the nutritions of the combined meal.
The third project was a composite interactive storyboard showing the history, relationship and popularities of major programming languages.

signature-attack.title: Adversarial Attack
signature-attack.subtitle: Black-box adversarial ML research, 2019
signature-attack.summary: A summer research on region-restricted black-box attacks against handwritten signature verification that later became a Pattern Recognition paper. I really only spent two weeks on foestering the major algorithms, but it worked and eventually got published.

preview.text: The work started from a course interest in adversarial examples. It focused on keeping perturbations inside the stroke region, so they stayed hard to notice and survived background cleaning.
You can find the paper here:

preview.label: Black-box attack against handwritten signature verification with region-restricted adversarial perturbations

noise-modulation.title: Noise Modulation
noise-modulation.subtitle: Interpretability and robustness study
noise-modulation.summary: An unpublished paper proposing a lightweight way to recover cleaner, more interpretable input gradients without full adversarial training.

preview.text: The idea was to multiply images with learned noise during training. That pushed the model to rely on the truly critical components, cleaning up the gradient signal at the cost of some accuracy.
The paper will never be published, it's archived here:

items: [Noise Modulation: Let your model interpret itself](https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.10603)

california-one-2022.title: California State Route 1
california-one-2022.subtitle: A coastal drive during the Verily summer
california-one-2022.summary: A Pacific coast travel node tied to the California internship period, with a scenic route through places like Half Moon Bay, Santa Cruz, Monterey, and 17-Mile Drive.

preview.text: Among the old travel artifacts, this one is especially visual. It reads more like a route sketch than a diary entry: coastlines, viewpoints, and a day-long line from the Bay toward Monterey during the [Verily internship](/nodes/verily-intern).
What stayed with me was not a single stop, but the feeling of the whole drive unfolding in sequence, almost like stepping through a living `storyboard` of California.

items: **Half Moon Bay** as the first clear transition out of the Bay rhythm
*Santa Cruz* as the midpoint where the coast started to feel more leisurely than logistical
**Monterey** as the emotional center of the trip
*17-Mile Drive* as the part that made the route feel intentionally scenic rather than merely efficient

puerto-rico-2022.title: Puerto Rico
puerto-rico-2022.subtitle: December 2022 island trip
puerto-rico-2022.summary: A late-2022 trip that feels like a marker between the Cornell chapter and what came next, with beaches, coastal stops, and a very different atmosphere from Ithaca.

preview.text: The old tripline artwork turns this into a vivid node: Old San Juan, Punta Higuero Lighthouse, beaches, airport thresholds, and a compact trip at the end of 2022 that feels transitional in the larger story.

items: Playa Escondida and other coastal stops
